Kimberly A. Pallen, Esq., Withers Bergman LLP 415.872.3200 kimberly.pallen@withersworldwide.com Attorneys for Defendant/Cross-Complainant,
Payward Inc.
Tamara Houston CSR#7244, 530-277-9916, tamarahouston.csr@gmail.com, Court reporter

Not contested. Not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PAYWARD, INC.'S Motion To Seal Certain Exhibits And Information Filed In Support Of Cross-Complainant Payward, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Adjudication

Payward, Inc.'s motion to seal is denied without prejudice.

To enable a court to make the specific findings required by California Rule of Court 2.550(d), a party moving to seal court records must present "specific enumeration of the facts sought to be withheld and specific reasons for withholding them." (See H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) Here, while portions of documents are laundry-listed, the specific facts sought to be withheld are not enumerated. Moreover, while broad reasons for withholding documents (e.g., "sensitive, confidential") are also laundry-listed, little or no attempt is made to link facts with reasons.

Order is signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Documents In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment On Plaintiffs Complaint, Or Summary Adjudication In The Alternative And In Support Of Cross-Complainant Payward, Inc.S Motion For Summary Adjudication On Its Cross-Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Jan-08-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Aaron P. Minnis, Esq. MINNIS & SMALLETS LLP (415) 551-0885 aaron@minnisandsmallets.com Attorneys for Plaintiff DANIEL TRAN for Plaintiff.
Kimberly A. Pallen, Esq., Withers Bergman LLP 415.872.3200 kimberly.pallen@withersworldwide.com Attorneys for Defendant/Cross-Complainant,
Payward Inc.
Tamara Houston CSR#7244, 530-277-9916, tamarahouston.csr@gmail.com, Court reporter

Not contested. Not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF DANIEL TRAN'S Motion To Seal Documents In Support Of Motion For Summary Judgment On Plaintiffs Complaint, Or Summary Adjudication In The Alternative And In Support Of Cross-Complainant Payward, Inc.S Motion For Summary Adjudication On Its Cross-Complaint

Plaintiff/cross-defendant Daniel Tran's motion to seal documents filed by Payward, Inc. in support of its motions for summary judgment and summary adjudication is granted.

Defendant Payward, Inc. (Payward) employed Tran from October 2021 to February 2022, when he was terminated. Tran brought a complaint for wrongful termination and statutory violations, and Payward brought a cross-complaint for breach of contract, breach of good faith and fair dealing, and misappropriation of trade secrets. Tran now files this unopposed motion to seal documents filed in support of Payward's motions for summary judgment and summary adjudication on the complaint and cross-complaint, respectively.

To order sealing, the court must find: (1) There exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) The overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 2.550(d).) Tran's request meets each of these criteria.

The constitutional right of privacy applies to a party's medical records. (John B. v. Superior Court (2006) 38 Cal.4th 1177, 1198.) Tran has an overriding interest in protecting his sensitive health information, which overrides the public's interest in the information. Tran's health information would be public if the records are not sealed, prejudicing his interest. And the request is narrowly tailored to health records in the filings, and there are no less restrictive means to protect Tran's interest.

The court grants the motion as to the memorandum, separate statement, and appendix for both the motion for summary judgment and the motion for summary adjudication

order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Tamara Houston CSR#7244, 530-277-9916, tamarahouston.csr@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Disqualify Plaintiffs Attorney Minnis
Off Calendar Oct-11-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. AARON P. MINNIS, Esq. of Minnis & Smallets LLP (Tel: 415-551-0885) for plaintiff;
Atty. KIMBERLY PALLEN, Esq. of Withers Bergman LLP (Tel: 415-872-3200) for defendant Payward, Inc.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT PAYWARD, INC.'s Motion To Disqualify Plaintiffs Attorney Minnis.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion to disqualify counsel is denied.

Defendant contends that plaintiff's counsel ("Minnis") should be disqualified because Mr. Minnis acted as a Judge Pro Tem in a discovery matter in Nathan Runyon v. Payward, Inc. dba Kraken et al. (CGC-19-581099) ("Runyon")

California Rule of Professional Conduct ("CPRC") 1.12(a) provides in pertinent part that "a lawyer shall not represent anyone in connection with a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer."

Here, movant fails to show that Runyon and this case involve the same "matter." "In determining whether two particular matters are the same, a lawyer should consider the extent to which the matters involve the same basic facts, the same or related parties, and the time elapsed." (Hassett v. Olson (2022) 78 Cal.App.5th 866, 877.) Here, while Runyon and this action involve the same defendant, the same basic facts of each case are entirely distinct. Each case involves a different employee and unrelated charges of statutory violations. The court further notes that the parties apparently agree that plaintiff Tran was not even an employee of defendant's when the Runyon action settled. (See Monument Builders of Pennsylvania, Inc. v. Catholic Cemeteries Assn., Inc. (1999) 190 F.R.D. 164, 166 ["the two actions should be treated as the same 'matter': they involve the same parties and largely the same facts and conduct" (emphasis added)].)

The court further rules that Mr. Minnis' participation in Runyon was not substantial. Comment 1 to the CPRC 1.12 provides: "Substantial participation requires that the lawyer's involvement was of significance to the matter. A finding of substantiality should be based not only on the effort devoted to the matter, but also on the importance of the effort." CRPC 1.01(l) provides: "'Substantial' when used in reference to degree or extent means a material matter of clear and weighty importance."

In this case, Mr. Minnis ruled on a single discovery motion regarding whether to compel the deposition of a third-party witness. The motion did not resolve a merits issue and defendant fails to demonstrate that Mr. Minnis' participation involved "a material matter of clear and weighty importance."

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Angela Pourtabib, CSR No. 13714, Email: apourtabib@gmail.com, Tel: 415-601-7054 (302/RBU)
